source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The biggest difference is speed. In the past, communication was
often slower, more deliberate, and sometimes more formal. People relied much
more on face-to-face conversations, landline calls, letters, or perhaps email later
on. Now, communication is instant. A message can be sent in seconds, a video
call can connect people across the world, and social media has turned
communication into something constant rather than occasional. In that sense,
we have moved from waiting for replies to almost living inside them.
[最大的分別在於速度。以前的溝通方式通常較慢、較經過思考，而且有時也較正式。人們
較多依靠面對面交談、家居電話、書信，之後才慢慢有電郵。現在，溝通幾乎是即時的。一個
訊息幾秒鐘就能送出，一個視像通話就能把相隔世界兩端的人連起來，而社交媒體更把溝
通變成一件持續進行的事，而不是偶爾發生的事。從這個角度來說，我們已經從「等回覆」的
年代，走到了幾乎像活在回覆之中的年代。]

components.english: For example, when my parents were younger, if they wanted to arrange something with a friend, they might have had to call the person at home or meet them in person. These days, people can sort out the same plan through a few quick messages, voice notes, or a group chat without much effort at all.
components.chinese: 例如，在我父母年輕的時候，如果他們想和朋友約時間，可能要打電話到對方家裡，或者直接見面談。但現在，人們只要透過幾個簡短訊息、語音訊息，或者一個群組對話，就可以很輕鬆地把同一件事安排好。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, when my parents were younger, if they wanted to
arrange something with a friend, they might have had to call the person at
home or meet them in person. These days, people can sort out the same plan
through a few quick messages, voice notes, or a group chat without much effort
at all.
[例如，在我父母年輕的時候，如果他們想和朋友約時間，可能要打電話到對方家裡，或者
直接見面談。但現在，人們只要透過幾個簡短訊息、語音訊息，或者一個群組對話，就可以
很輕鬆地把同一件事安排好。]

components.english: So yes, I would say the mechanics of communication have changed a great deal, mainly because technology has made everything faster, easier, and more immediate.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說溝通的運作方式的確已經改變了很多，主要是因為科技令一切都變得更快、更方便，也更即時。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say the mechanics of communication have
changed a great deal, mainly because technology has made everything faster,
easier, and more immediate.
[所以，是的，我會說溝通的運作方式的確已經改變了很多，主要是因為科技令一切都變得
更快、更方便，也更即時。]

components.english: That said, I do not think the deeper purpose of communication has changed nearly as much as the tools have.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為溝通更深層的目的，改變得和工具本身一樣多。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think the deeper purpose of communication has
changed nearly as much as the tools have.
[不過，我並不認為溝通更深層的目的，改變得和工具本身一樣多。]

components.english: People still communicate for the same basic reasons: to connect, to share feelings, to exchange ideas, to ask for help, and to feel less alone. The tools may have changed from letters to texts, from landlines to smartphones, but the human need underneath is still very much the same. In other words, the vehicle has changed dramatically, but the destination has not.
components.chinese: 人們溝通的基本原因其實仍然一樣：為了連結、分享感受、交換想法、尋求幫助，以及讓自己不要那麼孤單。工具也許已經從書信變成訊息，從家居電話變成智能手機，但底下那種人的需要其實仍然非常相似。換句話說，載體改變得很劇烈，但目的地並沒有變。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: People still communicate for the same basic reasons: to
connect, to share feelings, to exchange ideas, to ask for help, and to feel less
alone. The tools may have changed from letters to texts, from landlines to
smartphones, but the human need underneath is still very much the same. In
other words, the vehicle has changed dramatically, but the destination has not.
[人們溝通的基本原因其實仍然一樣：為了連結、分享感受、交換想法、尋求幫助，以及讓自
己不要那麼孤單。工具也許已經從書信變成訊息，從家居電話變成智能手機，但底下那種人
的需要其實仍然非常相似。換句話說，載體改變得很劇烈，但目的地並沒有變。]

components.english: For instance, people used to write letters or make long phone calls to stay in touch with family members. Today they may send photos, emojis, or short messages instead. The style is different, and the rhythm is different, but the heart of it is still the same: people want to reach one another.
components.chinese: 例如，以前人們會寫信，或者打很長的電話，去和家人保持聯絡。今天，他們則可能會傳照片、表情符號，或者短短的訊息。形式不同了，節奏不同了，但核心其實一樣：人們仍然想接觸彼此。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, people used to write letters or make long phone calls
to stay in touch with family members. Today they may send photos, emojis, or
short messages instead. The style is different, and the rhythm is different, but
the heart of it is still the same: people want to reach one another.
[例如，以前人們會寫信，或者打很長的電話，去和家人保持聯絡。今天，他們則可能會傳照
片、表情符號，或者短短的訊息。形式不同了，節奏不同了，但核心其實一樣：人們仍然想接
觸彼此。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say communication has changed hugely on the surface over the last few decades, but not so much at its core. We may speak through different screens now, but we are still trying to do the same old human thing: reach out and be understood.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說在過去幾十年裡，溝通在表面形式上確實改變得非常大，但在核心本質上卻沒有變那麼多。現在我們也許透過不同的螢幕說話，但我們其實仍然在做同一件很古老、很人的事：伸出手去連結別人，並希望被理解。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say communication has changed hugely on the
surface over the last few decades, but not so much at its core. We may speak
through different screens now, but we are still trying to do the same old human
thing: reach out and be understood.
[總結來說，我會說在過去幾十年裡，溝通在表面形式上確實改變得非常大，但在核心本質
上卻沒有變那麼多。現在我們也許透過不同的螢幕說話，但我們其實仍然在做同一件很古
老、很人的事：伸出手去連結別人，並希望被理解。]

question.english: Why do people prefer texting as compared to phone calls?
question.chinese: 為甚麼人們比起打電話，更喜歡傳訊息？

source_lines.text: Topic: Why do people prefer texting as compared to phone calls?
為甚麼人們比起打電話，更喜歡傳訊息？

components.english: I think people generally prefer texting because it is more convenient, less intrusive, and easier to fit into daily life.
components.chinese: 我認為，人們普遍更喜歡傳訊息，因為它比較方便、沒有那麼打擾別人，也更容易融入日常生活。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think people generally prefer texting because it is more convenient, less
intrusive, and easier to fit into daily life.
[我認為，人們普遍更喜歡傳訊息，因為它比較方便、沒有那麼打擾別人，也更容易融入日
常生活。]

components.english: A phone call demands your full attention there and then, whereas a message gives you a bit more breathing room. You can read it when you have a moment, think about your reply, and answer without dropping everything on the spot. In a fast-paced world, that kind of flexibility is gold dust. In a way, a call knocks on the door, but a text slips quietly through the letterbox.
components.chinese: 電話通常要求你當下立刻把注意力放上去；但訊息卻會給人多一點喘息空間。你可以在有空的時候才看、先想一想怎樣回覆，然後不需要立刻把手上的事情全都放下。在節奏很快的世界裡，這種彈性其實很珍貴。某程度上，電話像是在敲門，要你馬上應門；但訊息則比較像安靜地從信箱滑進來。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: A phone call demands your full attention there and then,
whereas a message gives you a bit more breathing room. You can read it when
you have a moment, think about your reply, and answer without dropping
everything on the spot. In a fast-paced world, that kind of flexibility is gold
dust. In a way, a call knocks on the door, but a text slips quietly through the
letterbox.
[電話通常要求你當下立刻把注意力放上去；但訊息卻會給人多一點喘息空間。你可以在有
空的時候才看、先想一想怎樣回覆，然後不需要立刻把手上的事情全都放下。在節奏很快的
世界裡，這種彈性其實很珍貴。某程度上，電話像是在敲門，要你馬上應門；但訊息則比較
像安靜地從信箱滑進來。]

components.english: For example, if someone is working, studying, commuting, or just not in the mood to talk, texting is much easier. They can reply a little later without turning the whole interaction into a big thing. That makes communication feel lighter and more manageable.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人正在工作、讀書、搭車，或者單純沒甚麼心情說話，那麼傳訊息就容易得多。他可以稍後再回覆，而不需要把整件事變成一個很正式的大互動。這也令溝通變得更輕鬆、更容易處理。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if someone is working, studying, commuting, or just
not in the mood to talk, texting is much easier. They can reply a little later
without turning the whole interaction into a big thing. That makes
communication feel lighter and more manageable.
[例如，如果一個人正在工作、讀書、搭車，或者單純沒甚麼心情說話，那麼傳訊息就容易得
多。他可以稍後再回覆，而不需要把整件事變成一個很正式的大互動。這也令溝通變得更輕
鬆、更容易處理。]

components.english: So, in short, people often choose texting because it gives them more control over their time, attention, and response.
components.chinese: 所以，簡單來說，人們之所以常常選擇傳訊息，是因為它讓他們對自己的時間、注意力，以及回覆方式有更多掌控。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in short, people often choose texting because it gives them
more control over their time, attention, and response.
[所以，簡單來說，人們之所以常常選擇傳訊息，是因為它讓他們對自己的時間、注意力，以
及回覆方式有更多掌控。]

components.english: On top of that, I think people also prefer texting because it feels less awkward and leaves a clear record of what was said.
components.chinese: 除此之外，我也覺得，人們喜歡傳訊息，還因為它比較不尷尬，而且能清楚留下說過甚麼的紀錄。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: On top of that, I think people also prefer texting because it feels less
awkward and leaves a clear record of what was said.
[除此之外，我也覺得，人們喜歡傳訊息，還因為它比較不尷尬，而且能清楚留下說過甚麼
的紀錄。]

components.english: Some conversations are easier to handle in writing than out loud. If a person feels shy, tired, or unsure what to say, texting gives them time to find the right words. It also avoids that slightly awkward dance of interrupting, talking over each other, or getting stuck in small talk. On top of that, messages leave a trail, so people can check details later instead of relying on memory alone.
components.chinese: 有些對話其實用文字來處理，會比直接說出口容易得多。如果一個人感到害羞、疲倦，或者不太確定該怎樣表達，傳訊息就能給他時間慢慢找對的字眼。它同時也避開了那種有點尷尬的來回，例如互相打斷、同時開口，或者卡在一些無謂的寒暄裡。除此之外，訊息會留下痕跡，所以人們之後還可以回頭看看細節，而不只是靠記憶。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: Some conversations are easier to handle in writing than out
loud. If a person feels shy, tired, or unsure what to say, texting gives them time
to find the right words. It also avoids that slightly awkward dance of
interrupting, talking over each other, or getting stuck in small talk. On top of
that, messages leave a trail, so people can check details later instead of relying on
memory alone.
[有些對話其實用文字來處理，會比直接說出口容易得多。如果一個人感到害羞、疲倦，或
者不太確定該怎樣表達，傳訊息就能給他時間慢慢找對的字眼。它同時也避開了那種有點
尷尬的來回，例如互相打斷、同時開口，或者卡在一些無謂的寒暄裡。除此之外，訊息會留
下痕跡，所以人們之後還可以回頭看看細節，而不只是靠記憶。]

components.english: For instance, if friends are arranging dinner, or colleagues are confirming a time and place, texting is often easier because everyone can check the message again later. In the same way, if someone wants to say something slightly sensitive, they may feel more comfortable putting it into words first rather than being caught off guard in a call.
components.chinese: 例如，如果朋友在約晚飯，或者同事在確認時間和地點，傳訊息通常會比較方便，因為每個人之後都可以再翻看一次。同樣地，如果一個人想說一些比較敏感的事情，他也可能會覺得，先用文字表達比在電話裡被逼著即時反應更自在。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if friends are arranging dinner, or colleagues are
confirming a time and place, texting is often easier because everyone can check
the message again later. In the same way, if someone wants to say something
slightly sensitive, they may feel more comfortable putting it into words first
rather than being caught off guard in a call.
[例如，如果朋友在約晚飯，或者同事在確認時間和地點，傳訊息通常會比較方便，因為每
個人之後都可以再翻看一次。同樣地，如果一個人想說一些比較敏感的事情，他也可能會覺
得，先用文字表達比在電話裡被逼著即時反應更自在。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say people prefer texting because it is flexible, low-pressure, and easy to manage. Phone calls still matter, of course, especially for urgent or emotional situations, but for everyday communication, texting simply fits modern life like a glove.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，人們偏好傳訊息，是因為它有彈性、壓力較小，而且容易處理。電話當然仍然有它的重要性，尤其是在緊急或者情感很重的情況下；但對日常溝通來說，傳訊息確實和現代生活貼合得非常自然。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d say people prefer texting because it is flexible,
low-pressure, and easy to manage. Phone calls still matter, of course, especially
for urgent or emotional situations, but for everyday communication, texting
simply fits modern life like a glove.
[總結來說，我會說，人們偏好傳訊息，是因為它有彈性、壓力較小，而且容易處理。電話當
然仍然有它的重要性，尤其是在緊急或者情感很重的情況下；但對日常溝通來說，傳訊息確
實和現代生活貼合得非常自然。]

question.english: Do you think modern technology has any negative influence on communication?
question.chinese: 你認為現代科技對溝通有負面影響嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you think modern technology has any negative influence on
communication?
你認為現代科技對溝通有負面影響嗎？

components.english: Yes, I definitely think it can, especially when it starts replacing face-to-face communication instead of simply supporting it.
components.chinese: 是的，我的確認為它可以有負面影響，特別是當它開始取代面對面溝通，而不只是輔助溝通的時候。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I definitely think it can, especially when it starts replacing
face-to-face communication instead of simply supporting it.
[是的，我的確認為它可以有負面影響，特別是當它開始取代面對面溝通，而不只是輔助溝
通的時候。]

components.english: The main reason is that technology often makes communication faster, but not always deeper. Messages can fly back and forth in seconds, yet tone, facial expression, and real emotional nuance can easily get lost along the way. As a result, people may be more connected on the surface, but less connected underneath. In that sense, modern communication can sometimes become a little thinner: quick to send, easy to miss, and surprisingly easy to misunderstand.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，科技很多時候令溝通變得更快，但不一定更有深度。訊息可以在幾秒鐘內來回傳送，但語氣、表情，以及真正細膩的情感層次，卻很容易在途中流失。結果就是，人與人表面上可能更容易連上線，但內裡卻未必更有連結。從這個角度來看，現代溝通有時會變得有點單薄：傳得快、漏得也快，而且很容易被誤解。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that technology often makes communication
faster, but not always deeper. Messages can fly back and forth in seconds, yet
tone, facial expression, and real emotional nuance can easily get lost along the
way. As a result, people may be more connected on the surface, but less
connected underneath. In that sense, modern communication can sometimes
become a little thinner: quick to send, easy to miss, and surprisingly easy to
misunderstand.
[主要原因是，科技很多時候令溝通變得更快，但不一定更有深度。訊息可以在幾秒鐘內來
回傳送，但語氣、表情，以及真正細膩的情感層次，卻很容易在途中流失。結果就是，人與人
表面上可能更容易連上線，但內裡卻未必更有連結。從這個角度來看，現代溝通有時會變得
有點單薄：傳得快、漏得也快，而且很容易被誤解。]

components.english: For example, two friends may argue over a text message simply because one sentence sounds colder or harsher than it was meant to. In the same way, a family may sit in the same room while everyone is glued to a different screen, so technically they are together, but not really communicating at all.
components.chinese: 例如，兩個朋友可能只因為一則文字訊息中的一句話看起來比較冷淡或比較刺耳，就產生爭執，儘管原意根本不是那樣。同樣地，一家人也可能坐在同一個房間裡，但每個人都黏在自己的螢幕上，所以從形式上看他們是在一起，實際上卻根本沒有真正交流。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, two friends may argue over a text message simply
because one sentence sounds colder or harsher than it was meant to. In the same
way, a family may sit in the same room while everyone is glued to a different
screen, so technically they are together, but not really communicating at all.
[例如，兩個朋友可能只因為一則文字訊息中的一句話看起來比較冷淡或比較刺耳，就產生
爭執，儘管原意根本不是那樣。同樣地，一家人也可能坐在同一個房間裡，但每個人都黏在
自己的螢幕上，所以從形式上看他們是在一起，實際上卻根本沒有真正交流。]

components.english: So yes, I would say modern technology can have a negative effect on communication, because it often increases speed and convenience while quietly reducing warmth, presence, and clarity.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說現代科技的確可能對溝通帶來負面影響，因為它很多時候在增加速度和便利的同時，也悄悄削弱了溫度、在場感，以及清晰度。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say modern technology can have a negative effect
on communication, because it often increases speed and convenience while
quietly reducing warmth, presence, and clarity.
[所以，是的，我會說現代科技的確可能對溝通帶來負面影響，因為它很多時候在增加速度
和便利的同時，也悄悄削弱了溫度、在場感，以及清晰度。]

components.english: That said, I do not think technology is the villain by itself, because it can also improve communication when people use it wisely.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不認為科技本身就是罪魁禍首，因為如果人們使用得當，它同時也可以改善溝通。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think technology is the villain by itself, because it
can also improve communication when people use it wisely.
[不過，我也不認為科技本身就是罪魁禍首，因為如果人們使用得當，它同時也可以改善溝

components.english: To be honest, modern life would be far harder without it. Technology allows people to stay in touch across countries, share information instantly, and maintain relationships that distance might otherwise weaken. Video calls, voice notes, and group chats can all be genuinely helpful. In other words, technology is a tool, not a personality. The real problem begins when people let convenience crowd out sincerity, or speed push out thoughtfulness.
components.chinese: 老實說，如果沒有科技，現代生活會難得多。科技讓人可以跨越國家保持聯繫、即時分享資訊，也能維持那些原本可能會被距離削弱的關係。視像通話、語音訊息，以及群組聊天，其實都可以非常有幫助。換句話說，科技是一種工具，而不是一種人格。真正的問題，是當人們讓方便把真誠擠走，或者讓速度把用心推開的時候。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: To be honest, modern life would be far harder without it.
Technology allows people to stay in touch across countries, share information
instantly, and maintain relationships that distance might otherwise weaken.
Video calls, voice notes, and group chats can all be genuinely helpful. In other
words, technology is a tool, not a personality. The real problem begins when
people let convenience crowd out sincerity, or speed push out thoughtfulness.
[老實說，如果沒有科技，現代生活會難得多。科技讓人可以跨越國家保持聯繫、即時分享
資訊，也能維持那些原本可能會被距離削弱的關係。視像通話、語音訊息，以及群組聊天，
其實都可以非常有幫助。換句話說，科技是一種工具，而不是一種人格。真正的問題，是當
人們讓方便把真誠擠走，或者讓速度把用心推開的時候。]

components.english: For instance, someone working overseas can still speak to their parents every day through video calls, which would have been much harder in the past. Likewise, classmates can coordinate projects quickly and efficiently online instead of wasting time chasing each other in person.
components.chinese: 例如，一個在外地工作的人，仍然可以透過視像通話每天和父母說話，這在過去其實是很難做到的。同樣地，同學也可以在網上快速而有效率地協調小組作業，而不用花很多時間到處追著彼此找人。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, someone working overseas can still speak to their
parents every day through video calls, which would have been much harder in
the past. Likewise, classmates can coordinate projects quickly and efficiently
online instead of wasting time chasing each other in person.
[例如，一個在外地工作的人，仍然可以透過視像通話每天和父母說話，這在過去其實
是很難做到的。同樣地，同學也可以在網上快速而有效率地協調小組作業，而不用花
很多時間到處追著彼此找人。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say modern technology does have some negative influence on communication, but only when people use it lazily or excessively. At its best, it brings people closer; at its worst, it leaves them talking more and connecting less.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說現代科技的確對溝通有一些負面影響，但那通常只會在人們使用得太懶散或太過度時出現。用得好，它能把人拉近；用得不好，它就會令人說得更多，卻連得更少。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say modern technology does have some negative
influence on communication, but only when people use it lazily or excessively.
At its best, it brings people closer; at its worst, it leaves them talking more and
connecting less.
[總結來說，我會說現代科技的確對溝通有一些負面影響，但那通常只會在人們使用
得太懶散或太過度時出現。用得好，它能把人拉近；用得不好，它就會令人說得更多，
卻連得更少。]

question.english: What do you think are some of the main differences between written communication and spoken communication?
question.chinese: 你認為書面溝通和口頭溝通之間，有哪些主要分別？

source_lines.text: Topic: What do you think are some of the main differences between written
communication and spoken communication?
你認為書面溝通和口頭溝通之間，有哪些主要分別？

components.english: I think the biggest difference is that written communication is usually more deliberate and lasting, whereas spoken communication is more immediate and flexible.
components.chinese: 我認為最大的分別在於，書面溝通通常更經過思考，也更具持續性；相反，口頭溝通則更即時，也更靈活。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think the biggest difference is that written communication is usually
more deliberate and lasting, whereas spoken communication is more immediate
and flexible.
[我認為最大的分別在於，書面溝通通常更經過思考，也更具持續性；相反，口頭溝通則更即時，也
更靈活。]

components.english: When people write, they usually have more time to choose their words, organise their ideas, and make the message clearer and more polished. Spoken communication, by contrast, happens in real time, so it is often less tidy but more natural. In that sense, writing is a bit like carving something into stone, while speaking is more like shaping water as it flows. One is slower and more fixed; the other is quicker and more alive.
components.chinese: 當人們寫東西時，他們通常有更多時間去選字、整理想法，並且把訊息表達得更清楚、更工整。相反，口頭溝通是在即時發生的，所以它很多時沒那麼整齊，卻更自然。從這個角度來說，書寫有點像把東西刻在石頭上，而說話則更像在流水之中塑形。一種較慢、較固定；另一種較快，也更有生命感。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: When people write, they usually have more time to choose their
words, organise their ideas, and make the message clearer and more polished.
Spoken communication, by contrast, happens in real time, so it is often less tidy
but more natural. In that sense, writing is a bit like carving something into
stone, while speaking is more like shaping water as it flows. One is slower and
more fixed; the other is quicker and more alive.
[當人們寫東西時，他們通常有更多時間去選字、整理想法，並且把訊息表達得更清楚、更工整。
相反，口頭溝通是在即時發生的，所以它很多時沒那麼整齊，卻更自然。從這個角度來說，書寫有
點像把東西刻在石頭上，而說話則更像在流水之中塑形。一種較慢、較固定；另一種較快，也更有
生命感。]

components.english: For example, if someone writes an email to a teacher or a manager, they will probably think carefully about tone, wording, and structure before sending it. But if the same person is talking face to face, they may speak more freely, change direction halfway through, or explain things in a less formal way.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人寫電郵給老師或上司，他多半會在發送之前仔細想好語氣、用字，以及整體結構。但如果同一個人是面對面說話，他就可能表達得更隨意一些，中途改變說法，或者用較不正式的方式去解釋事情。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if someone writes an email to a teacher or a manager,
they will probably think carefully about tone, wording, and structure before
sending it. But if the same person is talking face to face, they may speak more
freely, change direction halfway through, or explain things in a less formal way.
[例如，如果一個人寫電郵給老師或上司，他多半會在發送之前仔細想好語氣、用字，以及整體結
構。但如果同一個人是面對面說話，他就可能表達得更隨意一些，中途改變說法，或者用較不正
式的方式去解釋事情。]

components.english: So, one major difference is that written communication tends to be more planned and permanent, while spoken communication is usually more spontaneous and adaptable.
components.chinese: 所以，其中一個主要分別就是：書面溝通往往更有計劃性，也更持久；而口頭溝通通常則更即興，也更能隨情況調整。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one major difference is that written communication tends to
be more planned and permanent, while spoken communication is usually more
spontaneous and adaptable.
[所以，其中一個主要分別就是：書面溝通往往更有計劃性，也更持久；而口頭溝通通常則更即興，
也更能隨情況調整。]

components.english: Another important difference is that spoken communication carries much more through tone, facial expression, and body language, whereas written communication has to rely far more on words alone.
components.chinese: 另一個重要分別是，口頭溝通能透過語氣、表情，以及身體語言傳遞很多額外意思；相反，書面溝通則必須更多地依靠文字本身。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: Another important difference is that spoken communication carries
much more through tone, facial expression, and body language, whereas written
communication has to rely far more on words alone.
[另一個重要分別是，口頭溝通能透過語氣、表情，以及身體語言傳遞很多額外意思；相反，書面溝
通則必須更多地依靠文字本身。]

components.english: To be honest, when people speak, meaning does not come only from what they say, but also from how they say it. A pause, a smile, a raised eyebrow, or a gentler tone can change the whole feeling of a message. Writing does not have those built-in signals, so it needs to be clearer and sometimes more carefully structured to avoid misunderstanding. In other words, spoken communication has a voice behind it, while written communication has to build that voice on the page.
components.chinese: 老實說，當人們說話時，意思並不只來自他們說了甚麼，也來自他們是怎樣說的。一個停頓、一個微笑、一個揚起的眉毛，或者一種較溫和的語氣，都可能改變整個訊息的感覺。書面表達本身沒有這些天然訊號，所以它往往需要更清楚，也有時要更仔細地組織，才能避免誤會。換句話說，口頭溝通背後自帶聲音，而書面溝通則要靠文字自己把那把聲音建構出來。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: To be honest, when people speak, meaning does not come only
from what they say, but also from how they say it. A pause, a smile, a raised
eyebrow, or a gentler tone can change the whole feeling of a message. Writing
does not have those built-in signals, so it needs to be clearer and sometimes more
carefully structured to avoid misunderstanding. In other words, spoken
communication has a voice behind it, while written communication has to build
that voice on the page.
[老實說，當人們說話時，意思並不只來自他們說了甚麼，也來自他們是怎樣說的。一個停頓、一
個微笑、一個揚起的眉毛，或者一種較溫和的語氣，都可能改變整個訊息的感覺。書面表達本身
沒有這些天然訊號，所以它往往需要更清楚，也有時要更仔細地組織，才能避免誤會。換句話說，
口頭溝通背後自帶聲音，而書面溝通則要靠文字自己把那把聲音建構出來。]

components.english: For instance, if someone says “That’s fine” in a warm voice, it may sound genuinely reassuring. But if the same words appear in a message, the reader may not be sure whether the person is sincere, annoyed, or just being brief. That is why written communication can sometimes be neat on the surface but a little slippery underneath.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人用溫和的語氣說一句 “That’s fine”，那聽起來可能真的很有安撫作用。但如果同樣的字句出現在訊息裡，讀的人就未必能確定對方究竟是真心、帶點不滿，還是只是單純簡短。這也正正是為甚麼書面溝通有時表面上很整齊，底下卻有點容易滑出不同意思。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if someone says “That’s fine” in a warm voice, it may
sound genuinely reassuring. But if the same words appear in a message, the
reader may not be sure whether the person is sincere, annoyed, or just being
brief. That is why written communication can sometimes be neat on the surface
but a little slippery underneath.
[例如，如果一個人用溫和的語氣說一句 “That’s fine”，那聽起來可能真的很有安撫作用。但如果同
樣的字句出現在訊息裡，讀的人就未必能確定對方究竟是真心、帶點不滿，還是只是單純簡短。
這也正正是為甚麼書面溝通有時表面上很整齊，底下卻有點容易滑出不同意思。]

components.english: Overall, I think the main differences are that writing is usually more careful, structured, and lasting, while speaking is more immediate, expressive, and interactive. Both are important, but one is more like laying a thought down carefully, while the other is more like bringing an idea to life right in front of someone.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我認為兩者最大的分別，在於書面溝通通常更仔細、更有結構，也更具持續性；而口頭溝通則更即時、更有表達力，也更有互動感。兩者都很重要，只不過一種比較像把思想安穩地放下來，另一種則更像把想法直接活生生地送到別人面前。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I think the main differences are that writing is usually
more careful, structured, and lasting, while speaking is more immediate,
expressive, and interactive. Both are important, but one is more like laying a
thought down carefully, while the other is more like bringing an idea to life
right in front of someone.
[總結來說，我認為兩者最大的分別，在於書面溝通通常更仔細、更有結構，也更具持續性；而口頭
溝通則更即時、更有表達力，也更有互動感。兩者都很重要，只不過一種比較像把思想安穩地放
下來，另一種則更像把想法直接活生生地送到別人面前。]

question.english: What kinds of possessions are considered high-status to people in Hong Kong?
question.chinese: 在香港，人們普遍把哪些物品視為高地位象徵？

source_lines.text: Topic: What kinds of possessions are considered high-status to people in Hong
Kong?
在香港，人們普遍把哪些物品視為高地位象徵？

components.english: In Hong Kong, the possessions most often seen as high-status are usually prime homes, luxury watches, fine jewellery, designer handbags, and high-end cars.
components.chinese: 在香港，最常被視為高地位象徵的物品，通常是優質物業、名貴手錶、高級珠寶、名牌手袋，以及高級汽車。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: In Hong Kong, the possessions most often seen as high-status are usually
prime homes, luxury watches, fine jewellery, designer handbags, and high-end
cars.
[在香港，最常被視為高地位象徵的物品，通常是優質物業、名貴手錶、高級珠寶、名牌手袋
，以及高級汽車。]

components.english: The reason is that, in a place like Hong Kong, status is often tied not just to money, but to prestige, scarcity, and what people would call taste. A luxury flat suggests long-term success, while a serious watch or a hard-to-get bag works almost like a quiet badge. It says wealth, of course, but it also hints at access, familiarity, and belonging to a certain circle.
components.chinese: 原因在於，在香港這樣的地方，地位很多時不只是和金錢有關，也和聲望、稀有性，以及大家常說的「有品味」有關。一個高級單位往往象徵長遠的成功，而一隻像樣的名錶，或者一個不容易買到的手袋，很多時就像一枚低調的徽章。它當然在說明財富，但同時也暗示著圈層、門路，以及某種熟悉感。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The reason is that, in a place like Hong Kong, status is often
tied not just to money, but to prestige, scarcity, and what people would call
taste. A luxury flat suggests long-term success, while a serious watch or a
hard-to-get bag works almost like a quiet badge. It says wealth, of course, but it
also hints at access, familiarity, and belonging to a certain circle.
[原因在於，在香港這樣的地方，地位很多時不只是和金錢有關，也和聲望、稀有性，以及大
家常說的「有品味」有關。一個高級單位往往象徵長遠的成功，而一隻像樣的名錶，或者一個
不容易買到的手袋，很多時就像一枚低調的徽章。它當然在說明財富，但同時也暗示著圈
層、門路，以及某種熟悉感。]

components.english: For example, if someone lives in a sought-after area, wears a Rolex or Gucci, or carries a Hermès bag, many people would read those things as signs of success straight away. Even a premium car in Hong Kong can feel like more than transport; it can feel like a statement on wheels.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人住在受追捧的地段、戴著 Rolex 或 穿著Gucci，或者拿著 Hermès 手袋，很多人都會立刻把這些東西看成成功的象徵。即使是一部高級汽車，在香港也往往不只是交通工具，而更像是一種會移動的宣言。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if someone lives in a sought-after area, wears a Rolex
or Gucci, or carries a Hermès bag, many people would read those things as signs
of success straight away. Even a premium car in Hong Kong can feel like more
than transport; it can feel like a statement on wheels.
[例如，如果一個人住在受追捧的地段、戴著 Rolex 或 穿著Gucci，或者拿著 Hermès 手袋，很
多人都會立刻把這些東西看成成功的象徵。即使是一部高級汽車，在香港也往往不只是交
通工具，而更像是一種會移動的宣言。]

components.english: So, broadly speaking, high-status possessions in Hong Kong are usually the ones that combine price, visibility, and prestige.
components.chinese: 所以，整體來說，在香港被視為高地位象徵的物品，通常都是那些同時結合了價格、可見度，以及聲望的東西。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, broadly speaking, high-status possessions in Hong Kong are
usually the ones that combine price, visibility, and prestige.
[所以，整體來說，在香港被視為高地位象徵的物品，通常都是那些同時結合了價格、可見
度，以及聲望的東西。]

components.english: That said, I do not think status in Hong Kong always has to shout. For some people, the quieter possessions carry even more weight.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為香港的地位象徵一定都要很張揚。對某些人來說，那些比較安靜、低調的收藏，反而更有分量。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think status in Hong Kong always has to shout.
For some people, the quieter possessions carry even more weight.
[不過，我並不認為香港的地位象徵一定都要很張揚。對某些人來說，那些比較安靜、低調
的收藏，反而更有分量。]

components.english: Among the very wealthy, rare watches, art, fine wine, and collectible cars can matter just as much as flashy logos, because they suggest not only money but also knowledge and discernment. In other words, once people move beyond simply showing wealth, the real flex is often owning something that says, “I know what I’m looking at.”
components.chinese: 在非常富有的人之中，稀有手錶、藝術品、名酒，以及收藏級汽車，很多時和那些明顯的名牌標誌一樣有分量，因為它們展示的不只是金錢，還包括眼光和判斷力。換句話說，當一個人不再只是單純「曬有錢」時，真正高級的炫耀，往往是擁有一些能讓人看出：「我知道自己在看甚麼」的東西。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: Among the very wealthy, rare watches, art, fine wine, and
collectible cars can matter just as much as flashy logos, because they suggest not
only money but also knowledge and discernment. In other words, once people
move beyond simply showing wealth, the real flex is often owning something
that says, “I know what I’m looking at.”
[在非常富有的人之中，稀有手錶、藝術品、名酒，以及收藏級汽車，很多時和那些明顯的名
牌標誌一樣有分量，因為它們展示的不只是金錢，還包括眼光和判斷力。換句話說，當一個
人不再只是單純「曬有錢」時，真正高級的炫耀，往往是擁有一些能讓人看出：「我知道自己
在看甚麼」的東西。]

components.english: For instance, one person may show status with a designer bag, while another may do it with a painting, a wine collection, or a classic car that only enthusiasts would recognise. The first kind of status is easier to spot, but the second often feels more understated and, in a way, more powerful.
components.chinese: 例如，有人可能會用名牌手袋去展示地位；但也有人會透過一幅畫、一個酒藏，或者一部只有真正懂車的人才看得出價值的經典汽車去表達。前一種地位象徵比較容易一眼看穿；但後一種很多時反而更低調，也某程度上更有力量。

source_lines.text: 7. Example 2: For instance, one person may show status with a designer bag,
while another may do it with a painting, a wine collection, or a classic car that
only enthusiasts would recognise. The first kind of status is easier to spot, but
the second often feels more understated and, in a way, more powerful.
[例如，有人可能會用名牌手袋去展示地位；但也有人會透過一幅畫、一個酒藏，或者一部
只有真正懂車的人才看得出價值的經典汽車去表達。前一種地位象徵比較容易一眼看穿；
但後一種很多時反而更低調，也某程度上更有力量。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say high-status possessions in Hong Kong range from obvious luxury to quieter forms of prestige. In the end, what people admire is not just the object itself, but the story it tells about wealth, taste, and where someone seems to stand in the social pecking order.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說香港的高地位象徵，既包括一眼就看得出的奢華物品，也包括一些更安靜、更含蓄的聲望標記。說到底，人們欣賞的往往不只是物件本身，而是它背後所講述的故事——關於財富、品味，以及一個人似乎站在社會哪個位置。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say high-status possessions in Hong Kong range
from obvious luxury to quieter forms of prestige. In the end, what people
admire is not just the object itself, but the story it tells about wealth, taste, and
where someone seems to stand in the social pecking order.
[總結來說，我會說香港的高地位象徵，既包括一眼就看得出的奢華物品，也包括一些
更安靜、更含蓄的聲望標記。說到底，人們欣賞的往往不只是物件本身，而是它背後所
講述的故事——關於財富、品味，以及一個人似乎站在社會哪個位置。]

question.english: Were different possessions thought of as valuable in the past?
question.chinese: 在過去，人們會不會把不同的物品看成有價值？

source_lines.text: Topic: Were different possessions thought of as valuable in the past?
在過去，人們會不會把不同的物品看成有價值？

components.english: Yes, definitely. I think people in the past often saw very different possessions as valuable compared with people today.
components.chinese: 是的，絕對會。我認為過去的人，很多時候會把和今天的人不太一樣的物品視為有價值。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, definitely. I think people in the past often saw very different
possessions as valuable compared with people today.
[是的，絕對會。我認為過去的人，很多時候會把和今天的人不太一樣的物品
視為有價值。]

components.english: The main reason is that life used to be harder, slower, and less convenient, so people tended to value things that offered security, usefulness, or long-term stability. In many cases, possessions were judged less by style or novelty and more by whether they could feed a family, support a trade, or last for years. In other words, value used to sit more heavily in things that could carry real weight in everyday life.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，過去的生活比較艱難、比較慢，也沒有那麼方便，所以人們往往更重視那些能帶來安全感、實用性，或者長遠穩定的東西。在很多情況下，物品的價值不是看它是否時髦或新鮮，而是看它能否養家、幫助工作，或者用上很多年。換句話說，過去的價值感很多時候更落在那些能在日常生活裡真正派上用場的東西上。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that life used to be harder, slower, and less
convenient, so people tended to value things that offered security, usefulness, or
long-term stability. In many cases, possessions were judged less by style or
novelty and more by whether they could feed a family, support a trade, or last
for years. In other words, value used to sit more heavily in things that could
carry real weight in everyday life.
[主要原因是，過去的生活比較艱難、比較慢，也沒有那麼方便，所以人們往
往更重視那些能帶來安全感、實用性，或者長遠穩定的東西。在很多情況下，
物品的價值不是看它是否時髦或新鮮，而是看它能否養家、幫助工作，或者
用上很多年。換句話說，過去的價值感很多時候更落在那些能在日常生活裡
真正派上用場的東西上。]

components.english: For example, in the past, land, livestock, tools, sewing machines, sturdy furniture, or even a good watch might have been seen as extremely valuable. Those things were not just possessions; they were sources of income, survival, or reliability. A family might treasure a strong wooden table or a piece of farmland far more than people today would treasure the latest gadget.
components.chinese: 例如，在過去，土地、家畜、工具、縫紉機、耐用的家具，甚至一隻好手錶，都可能被看成非常有價值。這些東西不只是物品而已；它們同時也是收入、生活保障，或者可靠性的來源。一個家庭也許會比起今天的人珍惜最新電子產品，更珍惜一張結實的木桌，或者一塊農地。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, in the past, land, livestock, tools, sewing machines,
sturdy furniture, or even a good watch might have been seen as extremely
valuable. Those things were not just possessions; they were sources of income,
survival, or reliability. A family might treasure a strong wooden table or a piece
of farmland far more than people today would treasure the latest gadget.
[例如，在過去，土地、家畜、工具、縫紉機、耐用的家具，甚至一隻好手錶，都
可能被看成非常有價值。這些東西不只是物品而已；它們同時也是收入、生
活保障，或者可靠性的來源。一個家庭也許會比起今天的人珍惜最新電子產
品，更珍惜一張結實的木桌，或者一塊農地。]

components.english: So yes, I would say people in the past often valued possessions in a much more practical and long-term way.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，我會說過去的人很多時候是以一種更實際、也更長遠的方式去看待物品價值的。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, I would say people in the past often valued possessions in
a much more practical and long-term way.
[所以，是的，我會說過去的人很多時候是以一種更實際、也更長遠的方式去
看待物品價值的。]

components.english: That said, I do not think the difference is only about practicality; some possessions were also valued for emotional or symbolic reasons.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為差別只在於實用性；有些物品在過去也同樣是因為情感或象徵意義而被珍視。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think the difference is only about practicality;
some possessions were also valued for emotional or symbolic reasons.
[不過，我並不認為差別只在於實用性；有些物品在過去也同樣是因為情感或
象徵意義而被珍視。]

components.english: In the past, family heirlooms, jewellery, religious objects, and handmade items often carried deep personal meaning. They were sometimes treasured not because they were expensive, but because they held memory, identity, or family pride. So the real difference is not that people in the past cared only about survival. It is more that their idea of value was rooted more deeply in durability, meaning, and continuity. People often bought for life, not for the season.
components.chinese: 在過去，傳家之物、珠寶、宗教物件，以及手工製品，很多時候都帶有很深的個人意義。它們之所以被珍惜，有時不是因為特別昂貴，而是因為裡面裝著回憶、身份認同，或者家族的榮耀。所以，真正的分別不是說過去的人只重視生存，而是他們對價值的理解更深地紮根在耐用性、意義感，以及延續性之中。很多時候，人們買的是一輩子的東西，而不是一季的東西。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: In the past, family heirlooms, jewellery, religious objects, and
handmade items often carried deep personal meaning. They were sometimes
treasured not because they were expensive, but because they held memory,
identity, or family pride. So the real difference is not that people in the past
cared only about survival. It is more that their idea of value was rooted more
deeply in durability, meaning, and continuity. People often bought for life, not
for the season.
[在過去，傳家之物、珠寶、宗教物件，以及手工製品，很多時候都帶有很深的
個人意義。它們之所以被珍惜，有時不是因為特別昂貴，而是因為裡面裝著
回憶、身份認同，或者家族的榮耀。所以，真正的分別不是說過去的人只重視
生存，而是他們對價值的理解更深地紮根在耐用性、意義感，以及延續性之
中。很多時候，人們買的是一輩子的東西，而不是一季的東西。]

components.english: For instance, a grandmother’s ring, a hand-carved cabinet, or a pocket watch passed down through generations might have meant far more than its market price. Even if it was not flashy, it carried a story, and sometimes that story was worth its weight in gold.
components.chinese: 例如，一枚祖母留下來的戒指、一個手工雕刻的櫃子，或者一隻一代傳一代的懷錶，其意義很多時候都遠遠超過它本身的市價。即使它不算特別耀眼，它仍然承載著一個故事，而有時那個故事本身就貴重得像黃金一樣。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a grandmother’s ring, a hand-carved cabinet, or a
pocket watch passed down through generations might have meant far more than
its market price. Even if it was not flashy, it carried a story, and sometimes that
story was worth its weight in gold.
[例如，一枚祖母留下來的戒指、一個手工雕刻的櫃子，或者一隻一代傳一代
的懷錶，其意義很多時候都遠遠超過它本身的市價。即使它不算特別耀眼，
它仍然承載著一個故事，而有時那個故事本身就貴重得像黃金一樣。]

components.english: Overall, yes, different possessions were definitely thought of as valuable in the past. What people treasure always reflects the life they are living: in the past, people often valued what could last, serve, and anchor a family, whereas today many people are more likely to value what can connect, update, or impress.
components.chinese: 總結來說，是的，過去的人確實會把和今天不同的物品看成有價值。人們珍惜甚麼，其實往往反映了他們正在過怎樣的生活：在過去，人們很多時候珍惜的是那些能持久、能實用、也能穩住一個家庭的東西；而今天，很多人則更傾向珍惜那些能連結、能更新，或者能帶來印象的東西。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, yes, different possessions were definitely thought of as
valuable in the past. What people treasure always reflects the life they are living:
in the past, people often valued what could last, serve, and anchor a family,
whereas today many people are more likely to value what can connect, update, or
impress.
[總結來說，是的，過去的人確實會把和今天不同的物品看成有價值。人們珍
惜甚麼，其實往往反映了他們正在過怎樣的生活：在過去，人們很多時候珍惜
的是那些能持久、能實用、也能穩住一個家庭的東西；而今天，很多人則更傾
向珍惜那些能連結、能更新，或者能帶來印象的東西。]

question.english: Do advertisements give correct information, or do they encourage people to buy things that they may not need?
question.chinese: 廣告提供的資訊是正確的，還是它們會鼓勵人們去買一些自己未必需要的東西？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do advertisements give correct information, or do they encourage people
to buy things that they may not need?
[廣告提供的資訊是正確的，還是它們會鼓勵人們去買一些自己未必需要的
東西？]

components.english: Personally, I think advertisements usually contain some truth, but their real purpose is more to persuade than to inform, so they often push people towards buying things they may not actually need.
components.chinese: 就我個人而言，我認為廣告通常多少都包含一些真實資訊，但它們真正的目的與其說是提供資訊，不如說是說服別人，因此它們往往會把人推向購買一些自己未必真正需要的東西。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Personally, I think advertisements usually contain some truth, but their
real purpose is more to persuade than to inform, so they often push people
towards buying things they may not actually need.
[就我個人而言，我認為廣告通常多少都包含一些真實資訊，但它們真正的目的與其說是提
供資訊，不如說是說服別人，因此它們往往會把人推向購買一些自己未必真正需要的東

components.english: The reason is that advertising is designed to sell a product, not to give a perfectly balanced picture. Most advertisements shine a spotlight on the benefits and leave the drawbacks in the shadows. They often link a product to beauty, success, convenience, or happiness, so people begin to feel that buying it will somehow improve their life. In that sense, advertisements do not just sell objects; very often, they sell a feeling.
components.chinese: 原因是，廣告本來就是為了賣產品而設計的，而不是為了提供一幅完全平衡的全景圖。大多數廣告都會把優點照得很亮，卻把缺點留在陰影裡。它們常常把產品和美麗、成功、便利，或者快樂連在一起，於是人們就會開始覺得，買了那件東西，自己的生活好像也會跟著變好。從這個角度來說，廣告賣的不只是物件本身；很多時候，它真正賣的是一種感覺。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The reason is that advertising is designed to sell a product, not
to give a perfectly balanced picture. Most advertisements shine a spotlight on
the benefits and leave the drawbacks in the shadows. They often link a product
to beauty, success, convenience, or happiness, so people begin to feel that buying
it will somehow improve their life. In that sense, advertisements do not just sell
objects; very often, they sell a feeling.
[原因是，廣告本來就是為了賣產品而設計的，而不是為了提供一幅完全平衡的全景圖。大
多數廣告都會把優點照得很亮，卻把缺點留在陰影裡。它們常常把產品和美麗、成功、便利
，或者快樂連在一起，於是人們就會開始覺得，買了那件東西，自己的生活好像也會跟著變
好。從這個角度來說，廣告賣的不只是物件本身；很多時候，它真正賣的是一種感覺。]

components.english: For example, an advertisement for a new phone may make it look like a life-changing upgrade, even when a person’s current phone works perfectly well. In the same way, beauty or skincare ads may suggest that a product is almost essential, when in reality it may just be optional. That is how advertising can quietly turn a want into something that feels like a need.
components.chinese: 例如，一則新手機廣告可能會把那部手機塑造成改變人生的升級品，哪怕一個人現在的手機其實運作得好好的。同樣地，美妝或護膚廣告也可能讓某件產品看起來幾乎是不可缺少的，但實際上它可能只是可有可無。廣告很多時候就是這樣，安安靜靜地把一種「想要」變成一種看似「需要」。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, an advertisement for a new phone may make it look
like a life-changing upgrade, even when a person’s current phone works
perfectly well. In the same way, beauty or skincare ads may suggest that a
product is almost essential, when in reality it may just be optional. That is how
advertising can quietly turn a want into something that feels like a need.
[例如，一則新手機廣告可能會把那部手機塑造成改變人生的升級品，哪怕一個人現在的手
機其實運作得好好的。同樣地，美妝或護膚廣告也可能讓某件產品看起來幾乎是不可缺少
的，但實際上它可能只是可有可無。廣告很多時候就是這樣，安安靜靜地把一種「想要」變成
一種看似「需要」。]

components.english: So, if I had to choose, I would say advertisements more often encourage unnecessary buying than simply provide neutral information.
components.chinese: 所以，如果一定要二選一，我會說廣告更多時候是在鼓勵人們作出未必必要的消費，而不只是中立地提供資訊。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, if I had to choose, I would say advertisements more often
encourage unnecessary buying than simply provide neutral information.
[所以，如果一定要二選一，我會說廣告更多時候是在鼓勵人們作出未必必要的消費，而不
只是中立地提供資訊。]

components.english: That said, I would not say advertisements are always dishonest, because many of them do include factual information and can help people compare products.
components.chinese: 不過，我也不會說廣告一定都是不誠實的，因為很多廣告的確會包含事實資訊，也能幫助人們比較不同產品。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I would not say advertisements are always dishonest,
because many of them do include factual information and can help people
compare products.
[不過，我也不會說廣告一定都是不誠實的，因為很多廣告的確會包含事實資訊，也能幫助
人們比較不同產品。]

components.english: In many cases, advertisements tell people the price, the features, the function, or the promotion, and that can be genuinely useful. The real issue is not that advertisements are always false, but that they are selective. They put their best foot forward. So a thoughtful person can still learn something from an ad, provided that they remember it is a sales pitch, not a public service announcement.
components.chinese: 在很多情況下，廣告的確會告訴人們價錢、功能、用途，或者優惠，而這些資訊本身可以是真正有用的。真正的問題不在於廣告總是虛假，而在於它們往往是有選擇性的。它們總會把最好的一面擺在最前面。所以，只要一個人記得廣告始終是一種推銷，而不是甚麼純粹為公眾而設的資訊服務，他其實仍然可以從中得到一些有用的東西。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: In many cases, advertisements tell people the price, the
features, the function, or the promotion, and that can be genuinely useful. The
real issue is not that advertisements are always false, but that they are selective.
They put their best foot forward. So a thoughtful person can still learn
something from an ad, provided that they remember it is a sales pitch, not a
public service announcement.
[在很多情況下，廣告的確會告訴人們價錢、功能、用途，或者優惠，而這些資訊本身可以是
真正有用的。真正的問題不在於廣告總是虛假，而在於它們往往是有選擇性的。它們總會把
最好的一面擺在最前面。所以，只要一個人記得廣告始終是一種推銷，而不是甚麼純粹為公
眾而設的資訊服務，他其實仍然可以從中得到一些有用的東西。]

components.english: For instance, if someone is already planning to buy a washing machine, a laptop, or a pair of running shoes, advertisements may help them notice new models or compare features. However, the wise thing is to treat the advertisement as a starting point and then do a little more research before spending money.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個人本來就打算買洗衣機、手提電腦，或者一雙跑鞋，那麼廣告的確可以幫助他留意到新款式，或者比較不同功能。不過，更明智的做法，是把廣告當成一個起點，之後再多做一點資料搜集，才真正花錢。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if someone is already planning to buy a washing
machine, a laptop, or a pair of running shoes, advertisements may help them
notice new models or compare features. However, the wise thing is to treat the
advertisement as a starting point and then do a little more research before
spending money.
[例如，如果一個人本來就打算買洗衣機、手提電腦，或者一雙跑鞋，那麼廣告的確可以幫
助他留意到新款式，或者比較不同功能。不過，更明智的做法，是把廣告當成一個起點，之
後再多做一點資料搜集，才真正花錢。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say advertisements usually mix truth with persuasion. They can give some correct information, but they are far more interested in getting people to buy than in helping them think twice.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說廣告通常是真實資訊和說服技巧的混合體。它們的確可以提供某些正確資料，但它們更在意的，始終是讓人掏錢，而不是讓人三思。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d say advertisements usually mix truth with
persuasion. They can give some correct information, but they are far more
interested in getting people to buy than in helping them think twice.
[總結來說，我會說廣告通常是真實資訊和說服技巧的混合體。它們的確可以提供某些正確
資料，但它們更在意的，始終是讓人掏錢，而不是讓人三思。]

question.english: Where can people hear a lot of noise?
question.chinese: 人們通常會在哪些地方聽到很多噪音？

source_lines.text: Topic: Where can people hear a lot of noise?
人們通常會在哪些地方聽到很多噪音？

components.english: I’d say people can hear a lot of noise in busy public places, especially on main roads, at construction sites, in markets, and around transport hubs.
components.chinese: 我會說，人們通常會在繁忙的公共地方聽到很多噪音，尤其是大馬路、建築地盤、街市，以及交通樞紐附近。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I’d say people can hear a lot of noise in busy public places, especially on
main roads, at construction sites, in markets, and around transport hubs.
[我會說，人們通常會在繁忙的公共地方聽到很多噪音，尤其是大馬路、建築地盤、街市，以
及交通樞紐附近。]

components.english: The reason is quite obvious: those places bring together traffic, machinery, crowds, and constant movement all at once. When all of that piles up, the place can get noisy very quickly. In a big city, the background noise almost never really goes to sleep; it just hums, rattles, and rolls on.
components.chinese: 原因其實很明顯：那些地方同時集合了車流、機器、人群，以及不停的流動。一切堆在一起之後，地方很快就會變得很吵。在大城市裡，背景噪音幾乎從來沒有真正睡著；它只是一直低鳴、震動，然後繼續往前滾。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The reason is quite obvious: those places bring together traffic,
machinery, crowds, and constant movement all at once. When all of that piles
up, the place can get noisy very quickly. In a big city, the background noise
almost never really goes to sleep; it just hums, rattles, and rolls on.
[原因其實很明顯：那些地方同時集合了車流、機器、人群，以及不停的流動。一切堆在一起
之後，地方很快就會變得很吵。在大城市裡，背景噪音幾乎從來沒有真正睡著；它只是一直
低鳴、震動，然後繼續往前滾。]

components.english: For example, if you stand near a busy junction during rush hour, you may hear car engines, horns, people talking, buses pulling in, and sometimes even road works nearby. It can feel as though the whole city is speaking at once.
components.chinese: 例如，如果你在繁忙時間站在一個很忙的路口附近，你可能會同時聽到車聲、響號、人聲、巴士埋站的聲音，有時甚至還有附近的道路工程聲。那種感覺就像整座城市在同一時間一起開口說話。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if you stand near a busy junction during rush hour,
you may hear car engines, horns, people talking, buses pulling in, and sometimes
even road works nearby. It can feel as though the whole city is speaking at once.
[例如，如果你在繁忙時間站在一個很忙的路口附近，你可能會同時聽到車聲、響號、人聲、
巴士埋站的聲音，有時甚至還有附近的道路工程聲。那種感覺就像整座城市在同一時間一
起開口說話。]

components.english: So, in general, outdoor urban areas are probably the first places that come to mind when people think of noise.
components.chinese: 所以，一般來說，當人們想到噪音時，最先浮現的通常還是城市裡那些戶外公共空間。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in general, outdoor urban areas are probably the first places
that come to mind when people think of noise.
[所以，一般來說，當人們想到噪音時，最先浮現的通常還是城市裡那些戶外公共空間。]

components.english: That said, people can also hear a lot of noise indoors, not just outside.
components.chinese: 不過，人們聽到很多噪音的地方，其實不只是在室外，室內也一樣。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, people can also hear a lot of noise indoors, not just outside.
[不過，人們聽到很多噪音的地方，其實不只是在室外，室內也一樣。]

components.english: Places like schools, restaurants, shopping centres, and even crowded homes can be surprisingly noisy. Sometimes noise follows people indoors rather than stopping at the door. If there are many people in one space, with voices, music, phones, and activity all mixed together, the volume can rise before anyone even notices.
components.chinese: 像學校、餐廳、商場，甚至一些住得比較擠迫的家庭，其實都可以非常嘈。有時噪音並不會在門口停下來，反而會一路跟著人走進室內。如果很多人同時在一個空間裡，加上說話聲、音樂聲、手機聲，以及各種活動混在一起，音量往往會在大家還沒察覺之前就已經升上去了。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: Places like schools, restaurants, shopping centres, and even
crowded homes can be surprisingly noisy. Sometimes noise follows people
indoors rather than stopping at the door. If there are many people in one space,
with voices, music, phones, and activity all mixed together, the volume can rise
before anyone even notices.
[像學校、餐廳、商場，甚至一些住得比較擠迫的家庭，其實都可以非常嘈。有時噪音並不會
在門口停下來，反而會一路跟著人走進室內。如果很多人同時在一個空間裡，加上說話聲、
音樂聲、手機聲，以及各種活動混在一起，音量往往會在大家還沒察覺之前就已經升上去

components.english: For instance, a classroom full of excited children, or a packed restaurant at dinner time, can be every bit as noisy as a street outside. In the same way, if a family lives in a small flat with the TV on, people talking, and children running around, peace and quiet can quickly go out of the window.
components.chinese: 例如，一間坐滿興奮小朋友的課室，或者一間晚飯時間坐滿人的餐廳，都可以和外面的街道一樣嘈。同樣地，如果一家人住在比較小的單位裡，電視開著、大家在說話、孩子跑來跑去，那麼那種安靜感很快就會消失得無影無蹤。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a classroom full of excited children, or a packed
restaurant at dinner time, can be every bit as noisy as a street outside. In the
same way, if a family lives in a small flat with the TV on, people talking, and
children running around, peace and quiet can quickly go out of the window.
[例如，一間坐滿興奮小朋友的課室，或者一間晚飯時間坐滿人的餐廳，都可以和外面的街
道一樣嘈。同樣地，如果一家人住在比較小的單位裡，電視開著、大家在說話、孩子跑來跑
去，那麼那種安靜感很快就會消失得無影無蹤。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say people hear a lot of noise wherever there is heavy traffic, a lot of people, or constant activity. In other words, noise tends to gather wherever life is moving at full speed.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，只要一個地方有大量車流、很多人，或者很多事情同時在發生，人們通常就會聽到很多噪音。換句話說，哪裡的生活節奏開到最滿，噪音往往就會在哪裡聚集。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I’d say people hear a lot of noise wherever there is heavy
traffic, a lot of people, or constant activity. In other words, noise tends to gather
wherever life is moving at full speed.
[總結來說，我會說，只要一個地方有大量車流、很多人，或者很多事情同時在發生，人們通
常就會聽到很多噪音。換句話說，哪裡的生活節奏開到最滿，噪音往往就會在哪裡聚集。]

question.english: What may happen when someone listens to very loud music using headphones while they are running or hiking?
question.chinese: 當一個人在跑步或行山時，用耳機聽很大聲的音樂，可能會發生甚麼事？

source_lines.text: Topic: What may happen when someone listens to very loud music using
headphones while they are running or hiking?
當一個人在跑步或行山時，用耳機聽很大聲的音樂，可能會發生甚麼事？

components.english: I think it can be quite risky, because very loud music can block out important sounds and make a person much less aware of their surroundings.
components.chinese: 我認為這其實可以相當危險，因為很大聲的音樂會蓋過一些重要聲音，令一個人對周圍環境的察覺大大下降。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think it can be quite risky, because very loud music can block out
important sounds and make a person much less aware of their surroundings.
[我認為這其實可以相當危險，因為很大聲的音樂會蓋過一些重要聲音，令一個人對周圍環
境的察覺大大下降。]

components.english: When people are running or hiking, they need to stay alert. They may need to hear traffic, bicycle bells, warning shouts, approaching animals, or even the sound of their own footsteps on uneven ground. If the music is too loud, it can wrap them in a kind of sound bubble. In that state, they may miss something important and react too slowly.
components.chinese: 當人們在跑步或行山時，他們需要保持警覺。他們可能需要聽到交通聲、自行車鈴聲、別人的警告、接近中的動物，甚至自己踩在不平路面上的腳步聲。如果音樂太大聲，它就會把人包在一個聲音泡泡裡。在那種狀態下，他們可能會錯過重要訊號，反應也會變慢。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: When people are running or hiking, they need to stay alert.
They may need to hear traffic, bicycle bells, warning shouts, approaching
animals, or even the sound of their own footsteps on uneven ground. If the
music is too loud, it can wrap them in a kind of sound bubble. In that state, they
may miss something important and react too slowly.
[當人們在跑步或行山時，他們需要保持警覺。他們可能需要聽到交通聲、自行車鈴聲、別
人的警告、接近中的動物，甚至自己踩在不平路面上的腳步聲。如果音樂太大聲，它就會把
人包在一個聲音泡泡裡。在那種狀態下，他們可能會錯過重要訊號，反應也會變慢。]

components.english: For example, a runner may not hear a car horn when crossing a road, or a hiker may miss another person calling out that the path ahead is slippery or dangerous. A small delay like that can turn into a real accident.
components.chinese: 例如，一個跑步的人過馬路時可能聽不到車響號，或者一個行山的人可能聽不到別人提醒前面的路很滑或有危險。像這樣的小小延誤，有時就足以變成真正的意外。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, a runner may not hear a car horn when crossing a
road, or a hiker may miss another person calling out that the path ahead is
slippery or dangerous. A small delay like that can turn into a real accident.
[例如，一個跑步的人過馬路時可能聽不到車響號，或者一個行山的人可能聽不到別人提醒
前面的路很滑或有危險。像這樣的小小延誤，有時就足以變成真正的意外。]

components.english: So, one likely result is reduced awareness, and that can make outdoor exercise much more dangerous than it needs to be.
components.chinese: 所以，其中一個很可能出現的結果，就是警覺性下降，而這會令戶外運動變得比原本更危險。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one likely result is reduced awareness, and that can make
outdoor exercise much more dangerous than it needs to be.
[所以，其中一個很可能出現的結果，就是警覺性下降，而這會令戶外運動變得比原本更危

components.english: On top of that, listening to very loud music can also damage a person’s hearing and take away part of the enjoyment of the activity itself.
components.chinese: 除此之外，用很大聲的音樂也可能傷害一個人的聽力，並且削弱那項活動本身帶來的享受。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: On top of that, listening to very loud music can also damage a person’s
hearing and take away part of the enjoyment of the activity itself.
[除此之外，用很大聲的音樂也可能傷害一個人的聽力，並且削弱那項活動本身帶來的享

components.english: To be honest, ears are not designed to handle very loud sound for long periods, especially when people are already physically tired. Over time, that habit can lead to ringing in the ears, discomfort, or even hearing problems. At the same time, if someone is hiking in nature, blasting music into their ears may cut them off from the very thing they went there to enjoy. In other words, what should feel refreshing can become strangely numbing.
components.chinese: 老實說，耳朵並不是設計來長時間承受很大聲音量的，特別是在身體本來已經有些疲累的情況下。久而久之，這個習慣可能會導致耳鳴、不適，甚至聽力問題。同時，如果一個人是在大自然中行山，卻一直把很大聲的音樂灌進耳裡，那他也可能和自己本來去享受的東西隔絕開來。換句話說，一件本來應該令人感到舒暢的事，最後反而可能變得有點麻木。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: To be honest, ears are not designed to handle very loud sound
for long periods, especially when people are already physically tired. Over time,
that habit can lead to ringing in the ears, discomfort, or even hearing problems.
At the same time, if someone is hiking in nature, blasting music into their ears
may cut them off from the very thing they went there to enjoy. In other words,
what should feel refreshing can become strangely numbing.
[老實說，耳朵並不是設計來長時間承受很大聲音量的，特別是在身體本來已經有些疲累的
情況下。久而久之，這個習慣可能會導致耳鳴、不適，甚至聽力問題。同時，如果一個人是在
大自然中行山，卻一直把很大聲的音樂灌進耳裡，那他也可能和自己本來去享受的東西隔
絕開來。換句話說，一件本來應該令人感到舒暢的事，最後反而可能變得有點麻木。]

components.english: For instance, someone may finish a run feeling their ears are ringing, or come back from a hike without having noticed the wind, birds, or the general atmosphere around them. In that case, they may have had the music, but missed the moment.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人跑完步之後可能會覺得耳朵嗡嗡作響；或者行山回來時，根本沒有留意到風聲、鳥聲，或者周圍整體的氣氛。在那種情況下，他也許擁有了音樂，卻錯過了當下。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, someone may finish a run feeling their ears are
ringing, or come back from a hike without having noticed the wind, birds, or
the general atmosphere around them. In that case, they may have had the music,
but missed the moment.
[例如，一個人跑完步之後可能會覺得耳朵嗡嗡作響；或者行山回來時，根本沒有留意到風
聲、鳥聲，或者周圍整體的氣氛。在那種情況下，他也許擁有了音樂，卻錯過了當下。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say listening to very loud music on headphones while running or hiking can be both unsafe and unhealthy. Music can be a great companion, but when it is too loud, it stops being a soundtrack and starts becoming a blindfold for the ears.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說，在跑步或行山時用耳機聽很大聲的音樂，既可能不安全，也可能不健康。音樂本來可以是一個很好的陪伴，但當它太大聲時，它就不再只是背景配樂，反而更像是一塊蒙住耳朵的布。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say listening to very loud music on headphones
while running or hiking can be both unsafe and unhealthy. Music can be a great
companion, but when it is too loud, it stops being a soundtrack and starts
becoming a blindfold for the ears.
[總結來說，我會說，在跑步或行山時用耳機聽很大聲的音樂，既可能不安全，也可能不健
康。音樂本來可以是一個很好的陪伴，但當它太大聲時，它就不再只是背景配樂，反而更像
是一塊蒙住耳朵的布。]

question.english: What can be done to reduce traffic noise?
question.chinese: 可以做些甚麼來減少交通噪音？

source_lines.text: Topic: What can be done to reduce traffic noise?
可以做些甚麼來減少交通噪音？

components.english: I think one of the most effective ways to reduce traffic noise is through better urban planning and stronger control measures from the government.
components.chinese: 我認為，減少交通噪音其中一個最有效的方法，就是更好的城市規劃，以及政府更有力的管制措施。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think one of the most effective ways to reduce traffic noise is through
better urban planning and stronger control measures from the government.
[我認為，減少交通噪音其中一個最有效的方法，就是更好的城市規劃，以及政府更有力的
管制措施。]

components.english: The main reason is that traffic noise does not come out of thin air; it usually comes from too many vehicles, high speeds, constant honking, and roads that run too close to residential areas. If cities use sound barriers, quieter road surfaces, lower speed limits, and smarter road layouts, they can take the edge off the constant roar. In that sense, traffic noise should not just be accepted as the background music of city life. It can be turned down if the system is designed properly.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，交通噪音並不是無緣無故出現的；它通常來自太多車輛、過高車速、不停按喇叭，以及道路離住宅區太近。如果城市能使用隔音屏障、較安靜的路面物料、較低的車速限制，以及更聰明的道路設計，那麼那種持續不斷的轟鳴聲其實是可以被壓低的。從這個角度來看，交通噪音不應該只是被當成城市生活的背景音樂默默接受；只要制度設計得好，它其實是可以被調低的。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that traffic noise does not come out of thin
air; it usually comes from too many vehicles, high speeds, constant honking, and
roads that run too close to residential areas. If cities use sound barriers, quieter
road surfaces, lower speed limits, and smarter road layouts, they can take the
edge off the constant roar. In that sense, traffic noise should not just be accepted
as the background music of city life. It can be turned down if the system is
designed properly.
[主要原因是，交通噪音並不是無緣無故出現的；它通常來自太多車輛、過高車速、不停按喇
叭，以及道路離住宅區太近。如果城市能使用隔音屏障、較安靜的路面物料、較低的車速限
制，以及更聰明的道路設計，那麼那種持續不斷的轟鳴聲其實是可以被壓低的。從這個角度
來看，交通噪音不應該只是被當成城市生活的背景音樂默默接受；只要制度設計得好，它其
實是可以被調低的。]

components.english: For example, if a busy road runs near homes or schools, the government could install noise barriers, plant more trees along the roadside, and reduce the speed limit in that area. Even small changes like that can make the environment feel much calmer and less wearing over time.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一條繁忙道路靠近住宅或學校，政府就可以在那裡加設隔音屏障、在路旁種植更多樹木，並且降低該區的車速限制。即使只是這些看似不算很大的改動，長遠來說也能令整體環境平靜得多，沒有那麼折磨人。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if a busy road runs near homes or schools, the
government could install noise barriers, plant more trees along the roadside, and
reduce the speed limit in that area. Even small changes like that can make the
environment feel much calmer and less wearing over time.
[例如，如果一條繁忙道路靠近住宅或學校，政府就可以在那裡加設隔音屏障、在路旁種植
更多樹木，並且降低該區的車速限制。即使只是這些看似不算很大的改動，長遠來說也能令
整體環境平靜得多，沒有那麼折磨人。]

components.english: So, in my view, reducing traffic noise begins with treating it as a real urban problem rather than just something people are expected to put up with.
components.chinese: 所以，在我看來，要減少交通噪音，第一步就是把它當成一個真正的城市問題去處理，而不是當成市民理所當然要忍受的東西。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, in my view, reducing traffic noise begins with treating it as a
real urban problem rather than just something people are expected to put up
with.
[所以，在我看來，要減少交通噪音，第一步就是把它當成一個真正的城市問題去處理，而不
是當成市民理所當然要忍受的東西。]

components.english: On top of that, I think people themselves can also help by changing the way they travel and by driving more considerately.
components.chinese: 除此之外，我也認為，人們自己其實也可以透過改變出行方式，以及更有公德心地駕駛，去幫忙減少交通噪音。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: On top of that, I think people themselves can also help by changing
the way they travel and by driving more considerately.
[除此之外，我也認為，人們自己其實也可以透過改變出行方式，以及更有公德心地駕駛，去
幫忙減少交通噪音。]

components.english: To be honest, if more people use public transport, walk, or cycle when possible, there will simply be fewer vehicles on the road, and less noise follows naturally. At the same time, drivers do not need to lean on the horn at every minor delay, and motorbikes or modified cars that make an unnecessary racket should be controlled more strictly. In other words, quieter streets do not depend on infrastructure alone; they also depend on everyday behaviour. A city can build barriers, but people still need to stop adding fuel to the fire.
components.chinese: 老實說，如果更多人願意在可行情況下乘搭公共交通、步行，或者踩單車，路上的車輛自然就會減少，而噪音也會跟着下降。同時，司機其實也不需要每遇到一點小延誤就立刻狂按喇叭，而那些會製造不必要巨大噪音的電單車或改裝車，也應該被更嚴格地規管。換句話說，安靜的街道並不只靠基建本身；它同時也取決於日常行為。城市可以建隔音設施，但人也要停止繼續火上加油。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation: To be honest, if more people use public transport, walk, or cycle
when possible, there will simply be fewer vehicles on the road, and less noise
follows naturally. At the same time, drivers do not need to lean on the horn at
every minor delay, and motorbikes or modified cars that make an unnecessary
racket should be controlled more strictly. In other words, quieter streets do not
depend on infrastructure alone; they also depend on everyday behaviour. A city
can build barriers, but people still need to stop adding fuel to the fire.
[老實說，如果更多人願意在可行情況下乘搭公共交通、步行，或者踩單車，路上的車輛自然
就會減少，而噪音也會跟着下降。同時，司機其實也不需要每遇到一點小延誤就立刻狂按喇
叭，而那些會製造不必要巨大噪音的電單車或改裝車，也應該被更嚴格地規管。換句話說，
安靜的街道並不只靠基建本身；它同時也取決於日常行為。城市可以建隔音設施，但人也要
停止繼續火上加油。]

components.english: For instance, if commuters choose the MTR or buses instead of driving short distances, and if drivers become more disciplined about unnecessary honking, the difference may not feel dramatic overnight, but it can gradually make neighbourhoods much more liveable.
components.chinese: 例如，如果上班族願意改搭港鐵或巴士，而不是每一段短路程都開車，再加上司機在不必要的情況下少按喇叭，那種改變也許不會一夜之間變得很戲劇化，但它會慢慢令整個社區變得更宜居。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, if commuters choose the MTR or buses instead of
driving short distances, and if drivers become more disciplined about
unnecessary honking, the difference may not feel dramatic overnight, but it can
gradually make neighbourhoods much more liveable.
[例如，如果上班族願意改搭港鐵或巴士，而不是每一段短路程都開車，再加上司機在不必
要的情況下少按喇叭，那種改變也許不會一夜之間變得很戲劇化，但它會慢慢令整個社區
變得更宜居。]

components.english: Overall, I think traffic noise can be reduced through a mixture of better planning, stricter rules, and more responsible behaviour from road users. Traffic noise may never disappear completely, but if cities are designed more intelligently and people show a little more restraint and consideration, that endless roar can definitely be turned down.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我認為交通噪音可以透過更好的規劃、更嚴格的規則，以及道路使用者更負責任的行為來減少。交通噪音也許不可能完全消失，但只要城市設計得更聰明，而人也願意多一點克制和體諒，那種沒完沒了的喧鬧聲其實是可以被壓下來的。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I think traffic noise can be reduced through a mixture
of better planning, stricter rules, and more responsible behaviour from road
users. Traffic noise may never disappear completely, but if cities are designed
more intelligently and people show a little more restraint and consideration,
that endless roar can definitely be turned down.
[總結來說，我認為交通噪音可以透過更好的規劃、更嚴格的規則，以及道路使用者更負責
任的行為來減少。交通噪音也許不可能完全消失，但只要城市設計得更聰明，而人也願意多
一點克制和體諒，那種沒完沒了的喧鬧聲其實是可以被壓下來的。]

question.english: Do you think the world will be noisier in the future?
question.chinese: 你認為未來的世界會變得更嘈雜嗎？

source_lines.text: Topic: Do you think the world will be noisier in the future?
你認為未來的世界會變得更嘈雜嗎？

components.english: Yes, I think the world will probably become noisier in the future, or at least many parts of it will, especially large cities.
components.chinese: 是的，我認為未來的世界很可能會變得更嘈雜，至少很多地方會是這樣，尤其是大城市。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: Yes, I think the world will probably become noisier in the future, or at
least many parts of it will, especially large cities.
[是的，我認為未來的世界很可能會變得更嘈雜，至少很多地方會是這樣，尤其是大城市。]

components.english: The main reason is that modern life already moves at a fast pace, and it seems to be getting faster rather than slower. As cities grow, there is usually more traffic, more construction, more public activity, and more devices competing for people’s attention. On top of that, noise is no longer just physical. In a way, we also live in a storm of notifications, screens, and constant stimulation. So even when the ears get a break, the mind often does not.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，現代生活本來就節奏很快，而且看起來只會越來越快，而不是慢下來。隨著城市擴張，通常就會有更多交通、更多建築工程、更多公共活動，以及更多裝置在爭奪人們的注意力。除此之外，噪音也不再只是實體聲音。從某個角度來說，我們同時也活在一場由通知、螢幕和持續刺激所組成的風暴裡。所以，即使耳朵偶爾能喘一口氣，腦袋很多時候也未必能真正安靜下來。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that modern life already moves at a fast pace,
and it seems to be getting faster rather than slower. As cities grow, there is
usually more traffic, more construction, more public activity, and more devices
competing for people’s attention. On top of that, noise is no longer just physical.
In a way, we also live in a storm of notifications, screens, and constant
stimulation. So even when the ears get a break, the mind often does not.
[主要原因是，現代生活本來就節奏很快，而且看起來只會越來越快，而不是慢下來。隨著
城市擴張，通常就會有更多交通、更多建築工程、更多公共活動，以及更多裝置在爭奪人們
的注意力。除此之外，噪音也不再只是實體聲音。從某個角度來說，我們同時也活在一場由
通知、螢幕和持續刺激所組成的風暴裡。所以，即使耳朵偶爾能喘一口氣，腦袋很多時候也
未必能真正安靜下來。]

components.english: For example, in a busy urban area, people may wake up to traffic, hear drilling from nearby construction, sit in crowded transport, and then spend the rest of the day surrounded by phone alerts, background music, and advertising screens. When you add all of that together, silence can start to feel like a luxury.
components.chinese: 例如，在繁忙的城市地區，人們可能一早就被交通聲吵醒，又會聽到附近工程的鑽地聲，之後在擠迫的交通工具裡度過一段時間，然後整天都被手機提示聲、背景音樂，以及廣告螢幕包圍。當這些東西全都加在一起時，安靜就會開始變得像一種奢侈品。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, in a busy urban area, people may wake up to traffic,
hear drilling from nearby construction, sit in crowded transport, and then
spend the rest of the day surrounded by phone alerts, background music, and
advertising screens. When you add all of that together, silence can start to feel
like a luxury.
[例如，在繁忙的城市地區，人們可能一早就被交通聲吵醒，又會聽到附近工程的鑽地聲，
之後在擠迫的交通工具裡度過一段時間，然後整天都被手機提示聲、背景音樂，以及廣告螢
幕包圍。當這些東西全都加在一起時，安靜就會開始變得像一種奢侈品。]

components.english: So yes, overall I would say the world is likely to become noisier, because both our surroundings and our lifestyles are becoming more crowded and more demanding.
components.chinese: 所以，是的，整體來說我會認為世界很可能會變得更嘈雜，因為無論是我們身處的環境，還是生活方式本身，都正變得更擁擠，也更消耗人。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So yes, overall I would say the world is likely to become noisier,
because both our surroundings and our lifestyles are becoming more crowded
and more demanding.
[所以，是的，整體來說我會認為世界很可能會變得更嘈雜，因為無論是我們身處的環境，
還是生活方式本身，都正變得更擁擠，也更消耗人。]

components.english: That said, I do not think this trend is guaranteed everywhere, because people are becoming more aware of the value of quiet as well.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為這個趨勢在所有地方都一定會發生，因為人們同時也愈來愈意識到安靜的價值。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think this trend is guaranteed everywhere, because
people are becoming more aware of the value of quiet as well.
[不過，我並不認為這個趨勢在所有地方都一定會發生，因為人們同時也愈來愈意識到安靜
的價值。]

components.english: In some places, better urban planning, stricter noise control, quieter technology, and even remote working could reduce certain kinds of noise. For instance, electric vehicles are quieter than traditional ones, and some modern buildings are designed to block sound more effectively. In other words, the future may be louder in some ways but calmer in others. So the picture is not entirely black and white.
components.chinese: 在某些地方，更好的城市規劃、更嚴格的噪音管制、更安靜的科技，甚至遙距工作，都可能減少某些類型的噪音。舉例來說，電動車通常比傳統車輛安靜，而一些現代建築也設計得更能阻隔聲音。換句話說，未來也許在某些方面會更吵，但在另一些方面反而會更安靜。所以，整體圖像其實並不是完全非黑即白。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: In some places, better urban planning, stricter noise control,
quieter technology, and even remote working could reduce certain kinds of
noise. For instance, electric vehicles are quieter than traditional ones, and some
modern buildings are designed to block sound more effectively. In other words,
the future may be louder in some ways but calmer in others. So the picture is
not entirely black and white.
[在某些地方，更好的城市規劃、更嚴格的噪音管制、更安靜的科技，甚至遙距工作，都可能
減少某些類型的噪音。舉例來說，電動車通常比傳統車輛安靜，而一些現代建築也設計得更
能阻隔聲音。換句話說，未來也許在某些方面會更吵，但在另一些方面反而會更安靜。所以
，整體圖像其實並不是完全非黑即白。]

components.english: For instance, a person might live in a city that is busier than before, but if public transport becomes quieter, buildings become better insulated, and more people work from home, their daily sound environment may not feel quite as overwhelming. So progress can sometimes add noise with one hand and take it away with the other.
components.chinese: 例如，一個人也許住在一個比以前更繁忙的城市裡，但如果公共交通變得更安靜、建築物隔音更好，而且更多人改為在家工作，那麼他每天身處的聲音環境未必會顯得那麼令人吃不消。所以，進步有時候就像一隻手在增加噪音，另一隻手卻又在把它減走。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, a person might live in a city that is busier than before,
but if public transport becomes quieter, buildings become better insulated, and
more people work from home, their daily sound environment may not feel quite
as overwhelming. So progress can sometimes add noise with one hand and take
it away with the other.
[例如，一個人也許住在一個比以前更繁忙的城市裡，但如果公共交通變得更安靜、建築物
隔音更好，而且更多人改為在家工作，那麼他每天身處的聲音環境未必會顯得那麼令人吃
不消。所以，進步有時候就像一隻手在增加噪音，另一隻手卻又在把它減走。]

components.english: Overall, I’d say the world will probably become noisier, especially in urban and digital terms, but that does not mean people are powerless. In the end, whether the future sounds like chaos or balance will depend a lot on how wisely we design our cities, our technology, and our way of life.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我會說未來的世界大概會變得更嘈雜，尤其是在城市環境和數碼生活這兩方面；但這並不代表人們完全無能為力。說到底，未來聽起來會像一場混亂，還是一種平衡，很大程度上取決於我們是否有智慧去設計城市、科技，以及整體生活方式。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion 2: Overall, I’d say the world will probably become noisier,
especially in urban and digital terms, but that does not mean people are
powerless. In the end, whether the future sounds like chaos or balance will
depend a lot on how wisely we design our cities, our technology, and our way of
life.
[總結來說，我會說未來的世界大概會變得更嘈雜，尤其是在城市環境和數碼生活這兩方面
；但這並不代表人們完全無能為力。說到底，未來聽起來會像一場混亂，還是一種平衡，很
大程度上取決於我們是否有智慧去設計城市、科技，以及整體生活方式。]

question.english: What rules should be imposed to reduce noise pollution in the near future?
question.chinese: 在不久的將來，應該實施哪些規則來減少噪音污染？

source_lines.text: What rules should be imposed to reduce noise pollution in the near future?
在不久的將來，應該實施哪些規則來減少噪音污染？

components.english: I think stricter rules should be imposed on the main sources of noise, especially construction work, traffic, and loud commercial activities.
components.chinese: 我認為，應該對噪音的主要來源實施更嚴格的規則，特別是建築工程、交通，以及過於嘈吵的商業活動。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I think stricter rules should be imposed on the main sources of noise,
especially construction work, traffic, and loud commercial activities.
[我認為，應該對噪音的主要來源實施更嚴格的規則，特別是建築工程、交通，以及過於嘈
吵的商業活動。]

components.english: The main reason is that noise pollution may not look dramatic, but it can wear people down day after day. It affects sleep, concentration, mood, and even people’s general quality of life. So, in my view, governments should bring in clearer quiet-hour rules, tighter limits on late-night construction, and stronger control over unnecessary honking or excessively loud machinery. If a city never sleeps, that may sound exciting in theory, but in practice it can leave people feeling frayed at the edges.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，噪音污染也許看起來不算驚天動地，但它會一天天慢慢把人磨損掉。它會影響睡眠、專注力、情緒，甚至整體生活質素。所以，在我看來，政府應該制定更清晰的安靜時段規則、更嚴格限制深夜施工，以及更有力地管制不必要的按喇叭和過於嘈吵的機器。如果一座城市從不真正安靜下來，聽起來也許很熱鬧，但實際上卻很容易把人弄得心力交瘁。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that noise pollution may not look dramatic,
but it can wear people down day after day. It affects sleep, concentration, mood,
and even people’s general quality of life. So, in my view, governments should
bring in clearer quiet-hour rules, tighter limits on late-night construction, and
stronger control over unnecessary honking or excessively loud machinery. If a
city never sleeps, that may sound exciting in theory, but in practice it can leave
people feeling frayed at the edges.
[主要原因是，噪音污染也許看起來不算驚天動地，但它會一天天慢慢把人磨損掉。它會影
響睡眠、專注力、情緒，甚至整體生活質素。所以，在我看來，政府應該制定更清晰的安靜時
段規則、更嚴格限制深夜施工，以及更有力地管制不必要的按喇叭和過於嘈吵的機器。如果
一座城市從不真正安靜下來，聽起來也許很熱鬧，但實際上卻很容易把人弄得心力交瘁。]

components.english: For example, construction sites should not be allowed to drill or hammer late at night in residential areas, and drivers should be fined for using their horns carelessly. In the same way, shops or street promotions that blast music into the pavement should face stricter limits too.
components.chinese: 例如，在住宅區裡，建築地盤不應該被容許在深夜鑽地或敲打，而司機如果亂按喇叭，也應該被罰款。同樣地，那些把音樂大聲播到街上的商店或街頭宣傳活動，也應該受到更嚴格的限制。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, construction sites should not be allowed to drill or
hammer late at night in residential areas, and drivers should be fined for using
their horns carelessly. In the same way, shops or street promotions that blast
music into the pavement should face stricter limits too.
[例如，在住宅區裡，建築地盤不應該被容許在深夜鑽地或敲打，而司機如果亂按喇叭，也
應該被罰款。同樣地，那些把音樂大聲播到街上的商店或街頭宣傳活動，也應該受到更嚴格
的限制。]

components.english: So, one important step is to target the loudest and most disruptive sources of noise rather than just telling people to “be quieter.”
components.chinese: 所以，其中一個重要做法，就是直接針對那些最嘈、最打擾人的噪音來源，而不是只空泛地叫大家「安靜一點」。

source_lines.text: 4. Conclusion: So, one important step is to target the loudest and most
disruptive sources of noise rather than just telling people to “be quieter.”
[所以，其中一個重要做法，就是直接針對那些最嘈、最打擾人的噪音來源，而不是只空泛
地叫大家「安靜一點」。]

components.english: That said, I do not think the goal should be to make cities silent, because some level of noise is simply part of urban life.
components.chinese: 不過，我並不認為目標應該是把城市變得鴉雀無聲，因為某程度的聲音本來就是城市生活的一部分。

source_lines.text: 5. Idea 2: That said, I do not think the goal should be to make cities silent,
because some level of noise is simply part of urban life.
[不過，我並不認為目標應該是把城市變得鴉雀無聲，因為某程度的聲音本來就是城市生活
的一部分。]

components.english: What matters is balance. The best rules, in my opinion, would be practical rather than extreme. For instance, there could be lower noise limits around schools, hospitals, and residential neighbourhoods, while entertainment districts might be given slightly more flexibility, provided that venues install proper soundproofing. In other words, a city does not need to sound like a library, but it should not sound like a building site around the clock either.
components.chinese: 真正重要的是平衡。在我看來，最好的規則應該是實際可行的，而不是走向極端。舉例來說，學校、醫院，以及住宅區附近的噪音標準可以更嚴格；至於娛樂區，則可以保留稍多一點彈性，只要場所本身有做好隔音。換句話說，一座城市不需要安靜得像圖書館，但也不應該二十四小時都像個工地一樣。

source_lines.text: 6. Explanation 2: What matters is balance. The best rules, in my opinion, would
be practical rather than extreme. For instance, there could be lower noise limits
around schools, hospitals, and residential neighbourhoods, while entertainment
districts might be given slightly more flexibility, provided that venues install
proper soundproofing. In other words, a city does not need to sound like a
library, but it should not sound like a building site around the clock either.
[真正重要的是平衡。在我看來，最好的規則應該是實際可行的，而不是走向極端。舉例來
說，學校、醫院，以及住宅區附近的噪音標準可以更嚴格；至於娛樂區，則可以保留稍多一
點彈性，只要場所本身有做好隔音。換句話說，一座城市不需要安靜得像圖書館，但也不應
該二十四小時都像個工地一樣。]

components.english: For instance, bars, karaoke venues, and event spaces could be required to use better soundproof materials, while road planners could also build more noise barriers near busy highways. That way, the rules would reduce harm without sucking all the life out of the city.
components.chinese: 例如，酒吧、卡拉 OK 場所，以及活動場地都可以被要求使用更好的隔音材料；而道路規劃方面，也可以在繁忙公路附近增設更多隔音屏障。這樣一來，規則既能減少傷害，又不至於把城市原本的活力全部抽走。

source_lines.text: 7. Example: For instance, bars, karaoke venues, and event spaces could be
required to use better soundproof materials, while road planners could also
build more noise barriers near busy highways. That way, the rules would reduce
harm without sucking all the life out of the city.
[例如，酒吧、卡拉 OK 場所，以及活動場地都可以被要求使用更好的隔音材料；而道路規劃
方面，也可以在繁忙公路附近增設更多隔音屏障。這樣一來，規則既能減少傷害，又不至於
把城市原本的活力全部抽走。]

components.english: Overall, I think the most useful rules would be stricter quiet hours, tighter control of construction and traffic noise, and better soundproofing requirements for noisy businesses. The aim should not be perfect silence, but a healthier sound environment in which people can live, work, and rest without being constantly bombarded by noise.
components.chinese: 總結來說，我認為最有用的規則會是更嚴格的安靜時段、更緊的施工和交通噪音管制，以及對高噪音商業場所提出更高的隔音要求。目標不應該是追求絕對寂靜，而是建立一個更健康的聲音環境，讓人們能在不被噪音不斷轟炸的情況下生活、工作和休息。

source_lines.text: 8. Conclusion: Overall, I think the most useful rules would be stricter quiet
hours, tighter control of construction and traffic noise, and better
soundproofing requirements for noisy businesses. The aim should not be perfect
silence, but a healthier sound environment in which people can live, work, and
rest without being constantly bombarded by noise.
[總結來說，我認為最有用的規則會是更嚴格的安靜時段、更緊的施工和交通噪音管制，以
及對高噪音商業場所提出更高的隔音要求。目標不應該是追求絕對寂靜，而是建立一個更
健康的聲音環境，讓人們能在不被噪音不斷轟炸的情況下生活、工作和休息。]

question.english: What kinds of people are most likely to say sorry?
question.chinese: 甚麼樣的人最容易說對不起？

source_lines.text: Topic: What kinds of people are most likely to say sorry?
甚麼樣的人最容易說對不起？

components.english: I’d say the people most likely to say sorry are usually those who are empathetic, self-aware, and emotionally mature.
components.chinese: 我會說，最容易說對不起的人，通常都是那些有同理心、有自覺，而且情緒上較成熟的人。

source_lines.text: 1. Idea: I’d say the people most likely to say sorry are usually those who are
empathetic, self-aware, and emotionally mature.
[我會說，最容易說對不起的人，通常都是那些有同理心、有自覺，而且情緒上較成熟的

components.english: The main reason is that they are able to look beyond their own intention and think about the effect of their words or actions on someone else. They do not see an apology as losing face; they see it as putting things right. In that sense, saying sorry is not a weakness to them. It is more like social glue: it helps repair small cracks before they turn into bigger ones.
components.chinese: 主要原因是，這類人能夠跳出自己的立場，不只是看自己原本有沒有惡意，而是會想到自己的說話或行為對別人造成了甚麼影響。他們不會把道歉看成丟臉，反而會把它看成把事情重新放回正軌。從這個角度來說，對他們而言，道歉不是軟弱，而更像一種社交上的黏合劑：它能在小裂縫變成大問題之前，先把關係補起來。

source_lines.text: 2. Explanation: The main reason is that they are able to look beyond their own
intention and think about the effect of their words or actions on someone else.
They do not see an apology as losing face; they see it as putting things right. In
that sense, saying sorry is not a weakness to them. It is more like social glue: it
helps repair small cracks before they turn into bigger ones.
[主要原因是，這類人能夠跳出自己的立場，不只是看自己原本有沒有惡意，而是會想到自
己的說話或行為對別人造成了甚麼影響。他們不會把道歉看成丟臉，反而會把它看成把事
情重新放回正軌。從這個角度來說，對他們而言，道歉不是軟弱，而更像一種社交上的黏合
劑：它能在小裂縫變成大問題之前，先把關係補起來。]

components.english: For example, if an emotionally mature person interrupts someone in a meeting or speaks too sharply in an argument, they are more likely to notice it afterwards and say sorry without needing to be pushed. They may still have their own point of view, but they are willing to admit when their behaviour has crossed a line.
components.chinese: 例如，如果一個情緒較成熟的人在會議中打斷了別人，或者在爭論中說得太重，他往往較容易在事後察覺到，並且主動道歉，而不用等別人逼他。他也許仍然有自己的立場，但他願意承認自己的行為有越界的地方。

source_lines.text: 3. Example: For example, if an emotionally mature person interrupts someone in
a meeting or speaks too sharply in an argument, they are more likely to notice it
afterwards and say sorry without needing to be pushed. They may still have
